verses.text: 'Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth'; let another praise you, not your own mouth; a fool's wrath is heavier than stone and sand. The future is uncertain. Leave praise to others and bear no fool's grudge.

'Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth'; let another praise you, not your own mouth; a fool's wrath is heavier than stone and sand. The future is uncertain. Leave praise to others and bear no fool's grudge.

'Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth'; let another praise you, not your own mouth; a fool's wrath is heavier than stone and sand. The future is uncertain. Leave praise to others and bear no fool's grudge.

Wrath is cruel and anger outrageous, but who can stand before envy? Open rebuke is better than secret love; 'faithful are the wounds of a friend; but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.' Honest rebuke is love. A true friend wounds to heal; a foe flatters to harm.

Wrath is cruel and anger outrageous, but who can stand before envy? Open rebuke is better than secret love; 'faithful are the wounds of a friend; but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.' Honest rebuke is love. A true friend wounds to heal; a foe flatters to harm.

The full soul loathes the honeycomb, but to the hungry every bitter thing is sweet; a man who wanders from his place is like a straying bird; ointment and perfume rejoice the heart, so does a friend's hearty counsel. Need changes our taste. Sweet counsel from a friend gladdens the soul.

The full soul loathes the honeycomb, but to the hungry every bitter thing is sweet; a man who wanders from his place is like a straying bird; ointment and perfume rejoice the heart, so does a friend's hearty counsel. Need changes our taste. Sweet counsel from a friend gladdens the soul.

The full soul loathes the honeycomb, but to the hungry every bitter thing is sweet; a man who wanders from his place is like a straying bird; ointment and perfume rejoice the heart, so does a friend's hearty counsel. Need changes our taste. Sweet counsel from a friend gladdens the soul.

Forsake not your own or your father's friend, for a near neighbour is better than a far brother in trouble; be wise, my son, and answer my reproachers; the prudent foresee evil and hide. Value faithful friends. The wise see danger and take cover.

Forsake not your own or your father's friend, for a near neighbour is better than a far brother in trouble; be wise, my son, and answer my reproachers; the prudent foresee evil and hide. Value faithful friends. The wise see danger and take cover.

Take the garment of him who is surety for a stranger; loud early blessing is counted a curse; 'a continual dropping in a very rainy day and a contentious woman are alike,' impossible to restrain. Beware rash pledges. Endless strife wears down like a leaking roof.

'Iron sharpeneth iron; so a man sharpeneth the countenance of his friend'; he that keeps the fig tree eats its fruit; as in water face answers to face, so the heart of man to man. Friends sharpen one another. Our hearts mirror what we truly are.

Hell and destruction are never full, nor are man's eyes ever satisfied; the furnace tries silver, and a man is tried by his praise; 'though thou shouldest bray a fool in a mortar... yet will not his foolishness depart.' Desire is bottomless. Folly clings through the harshest treatment.

Be diligent to know the state of your flocks, for riches are not for ever; when the hay appears and herbs are gathered, the lambs and goats supply clothing and food, and milk enough for your household. Tend your work faithfully. Honest husbandry, not hoarded wealth, provides for the home.
